Pearson Edexcel
                                           Mark Scheme
                               Predicted Paper · For revision purposes only
                               Level 3 GCE Economics A (9EC0)
               Paper 1: Markets and Business Behaviour · 9EC0/01 · Advanced
                                        Total: 100 marks

General Marking Guidance
   • All candidates must receive the same treatment. Mark schemes should be applied positively,
     rewarding candidates for what they have shown they can do.
   • All marks on the mark scheme are designed to be awarded. Examiners should always award
     full marks if deserved.
   • Crossed-out work should be marked UNLESS the candidate has replaced it with an
     alternative response.
   • Accept any equivalent wording and alternative valid approaches not explicitly listed in the
     indicative content.

Assessment Objectives
AO1 Demonstrate knowledge of terms, concepts, theories and models.
AO2 Apply knowledge and understanding to various economic contexts.
AO3 Analyse issues within economics, using chains of reasoning.
AO4 Evaluate economic arguments and use evidence to support informed judgements.
Marking grids are organised as KAA (AO1+AO2+AO3) and Evaluation (AO4).

Level Descriptors
8-mark "Examine" (KAA = 5, Evaluation = 3)
 Leve    KAA marks                                       Descriptor
   l
  L1          1–2        Isolated element of knowledge; limited application.
  L2          3–4        Sound knowledge applied with some development; at least one chain of
                         reasoning.
  L3           5         Accurate detailed knowledge fully applied; developed chains.


10-mark "Assess" (KAA = 6, Evaluation = 4)
 Leve    KAA marks                                       Descriptor
   l
  L1          1–2        Limited knowledge; weak application.
  L2          3–4        Sound theory applied to context.
  L3          5–6        Accurate detailed theory, well applied; developed chains.


12-mark "Discuss with diagram" (KAA = 8, Evaluation = 4)
 Leve    KAA marks                                       Descriptor
   l
  L1          1–2        Limited knowledge; weak or missing diagram.
  L2          3–5        Sound knowledge; reasonable diagram; some application.

 Leve    KAA marks                                     Descriptor

 Leve    KAA marks                                     Descriptor
   l
  L1          1–3        Limited knowledge; weak application.
  L2          4–6        Sound knowledge with application; some development.
  L3          7–9        Accurate detailed knowledge; developed chains; relevant diagrams.


25-mark Essay "Evaluate" (KAA = 15, Evaluation = 10)
 Leve    KAA marks                                     Descriptor
   l
  L1          1–5        Limited knowledge; basic theory.
  L2          6–9        Sound theory applied to context.
  L3        10–12        Accurate detailed theory, well applied; relevant diagrams.
  L4        13–15        Comprehensive, sophisticated theory; diagrams used analytically.


25-mark Essay evaluation grid (AO4)
 Leve    Eval marks                                    Descriptor
   l
  L1          1–3        Assertion without support.
  L2          4–6        Supported evaluation; some judgement.
  L3          7–8        Developed evaluation; reasoned judgement.
  L4         9–10        Sophisticated evaluation with prioritised judgement.


9EC0/01 · Advanced · Mark Scheme                                                      Page 2 of 10
                                   Section A · Questions 1 to 5
Question 1 (5 marks) · Premium ride-hailing market
   Q                                     Indicative content                                 Mark
                                                                                             s
 1(a)    Demand shift: +9 at every price. New Qd column: 37, 43, 49, 55, 61 ('000            4
         trips).
         Supply shift: raise price at every Qs by £3 → effective Qs at given price falls.
         New effective Qs at price P equals the original Qs at price (P − 3). New Qs
         column: 46, 40, · (need to interpolate for prices between), 34, 28… Working:
         at £24, new Qs equals original Qs at £21 = 46. At £21, new Qs = original Qs at
         £18 = 40. At £18, new Qs = original Qs at £15 = 34. At £15, new Qs = original
         Qs at £12 = 28.
         New equilibrium: compare new Qd and new Qs. At P=£21: Qd=43, Qs=40
         (shortage of 3). At P=£24: Qd=37, Qs=46 (surplus of 9). Interpolating, Qd = Qs
         at P ≈ £21.75, Q ≈ 41,500 trips.
         Award 1 mark demand shift; 1 mark supply shift; 1 mark interpolation; 1 mark
         valid P and Q with working. Accept equilibrium price in range £21.50–£22.00
         and Q 41,000–42,000.

 1(b)    Answer: C · rise by approximately 5% (1).                                           1
         Working: with PED = −0.67, a 15% price rise causes a fall in Q of 0.67 × 15 =
         10%. Revenue change ≈ %ΔP + %ΔQ = +15% − 10% = +5%. Because
         demand is inelastic, total revenue rises when price rises.


Question 2 (5 marks) · SVOD cross-elasticity
   Q                                     Indicative content                                 Mark
                                                                                             s
 2(a)    Method: XED = %ΔQd(A) / %ΔP(B), so %ΔQd(A) = XED × %ΔP(B) (1).                      2
         = +0.3 × 10% = +3.0%. Accept +3% (1). Demand for SVOD rises by
         approximately 3%.

 2(b)    A negative XED (−0.6) indicates the two goods are complements (1) ·                 2
         consumed together; when broadband price rises, demand for SVOD falls
         because broadband is necessary to access streaming services (1).
         Accept alternative chains referencing derived demand: SVOD demand is
         derived in part from access to broadband infrastructure.

 2(c)    Answer: B · fall by 3%. Expected %ΔQd(SVOD) = XED × %ΔP(broadband) =                1
         −0.6 × (+5%) = −3%.
         Distractors: A has the correct magnitude but the wrong sign · negative XED
         means the two goods move together (both fall when broadband rises); C and D
         apply the XED coefficient to itself rather than multiplying by the 5% price
         change.


Question 3 (5 marks) · Platform price cap
   Q                                     Indicative content                                 Mark
                                                                                             s
 3(a)    Diagram: downward-sloping AR and MR; AC and MC curves. Show profit-max              2


9EC0/01 · Advanced · Mark Scheme                                                      Page 3 of 10
   Q                                    Indicative content                                     Mark
                                                                                                s
         P₁ at MR=MC with supernormal profit rectangle (P₁ − AC) × Q₁ (1). Insert
         horizontal price cap at P_cap < P₁; new effective demand curve is horizontal at
         P_cap up to demand curve. Show new output Q₂ > Q₁; smaller profit rectangle
         (P_cap − AC) × Q₂ (1).
         Accept labelled diagram with clear indication of reduced supernormal profit.

 3(b)    The platform's profit-maximising price falls from P₁ (the unconstrained                1
  (i)    monopoly price) to P_cap, since the cap is binding. Award 1 mark for correctly
         identifying a fall in price (accept 'decrease', 'lower', or equivalent).

 3(b)    Consumer surplus rises. With a lower price and a higher quantity traded at the         1
  (ii)   cap, the area under the demand curve and above price expands. Award 1
         mark for correctly identifying a rise in consumer surplus (accept 'increase' or
         equivalent).

 3(c)    Answer: B · reduce price, raise output and reduce deadweight loss (1). A cap           1
         set below the monopolist's profit-max price but above AC moves the market
         towards allocative efficiency.


Question 4 (5 marks) · Kinked demand and tacit collusion
   Q                                    Indicative content                                     Mark
                                                                                                s
 4(a)    Diagram: demand curve with a kink at the current price P*; relatively elastic          4
         above P* (rivals don't follow price rises), relatively inelastic below P* (rivals
         match price cuts) (1). Associated MR curve with a vertical discontinuity below
         the kink (1).
         Explanation: a price rise is not matched → firm loses large share (elastic
         segment); a price cut is matched → firm gains little but triggers a price war
         (inelastic segment) (1). Hence firms avoid changing price and compete on non-
         price dimensions (advertising, quality, loyalty) (1).

 4(b)    Answer: B · few firms, high entry barriers, and easily observable prices.              1
         These conditions support cartel-like outcomes without explicit agreement.


Question 5 (5 marks) · AI Compute Fund Pigouvian subsidy
   Q                                    Indicative content                                     Mark
                                                                                                s
 5(a)    Answer: B · positive externalities in consumption or production (1). Pigouvian         1
         subsidies align private benefit with higher social benefit, moving output toward
         the social optimum.

 5(b)    Definition/application: subsidy shifts the supply curve (or private MC) down by        2
         the size of the external benefit (1). At the new equilibrium, private output equals
         the socially optimal output where MPB + external benefit = MSC; welfare loss
         is eliminated (1).
         Accept a clearly labelled diagram showing shift of S (or MPC) downwards to
         coincide with MSC, new Q* = Q_social.

 5(c)    Subsidy per MW = £1,500,000,000 / 3,000 MW = £500,000 per MW = £0.5                    2


9EC0/01 · Advanced · Mark Scheme                                                         Page 4 of 10
   Q                                   Indicative content                                 Mark
                                                                                           s
         million/MW.
         1 mark for method (divide fund by capacity); 1 mark for correct numerical
         answer with unit.


9EC0/01 · Advanced · Mark Scheme                                                     Page 5 of 10
           Section B · Question 6: Digital platforms and advertising
6(a) · Why digital markets tend to 'winner-takes-most' (5 marks)
Indicative content (KAA 4 / Eval 1):
Network effects · value of platform rises with number of users and complementors (Extract A):
91.2% Google share in search and 76.4% YouTube share in online video show clear tipping (Fig 1)
(2). Very low marginal costs of serving additional users, combined with large fixed/sunk costs in
data centres and R&D, mean average cost falls sharply with scale (economies of scale → natural-
monopoly-like tendencies) (2). Proprietary user-behavioural data creates a feedback loop that
reinforces dominance (1).
Evaluation (1): tipping is not inevitable · e.g. social/mobile OS duopoly (Apple iOS 52.8% with
iOS + Android ≈ 100%) shows markets can also settle on stable oligopoly. Context matters.

6(b) · Consumer-welfare effects of high concentration in digital advertising
(12 marks)
Diagram required: cost/revenue diagram for a monopolistically-competitive or dominant-firm
advertising platform; show P > MC, supernormal profit, deadweight loss area.

            KAA (AO1 + AO2 + AO3)                              Evaluation (AO4)

 • Figure 2: Google (39.7%) + Meta (24.2%) =      • Zero-price model makes traditional welfare
   CR2 of 63.9%; top 3 = 75.3%. Clear               measurement difficult · consumer harm is
   duopoly of digital ad spend.                     non-pecuniary (privacy, attention).
 • Concentration allows platforms to charge       • Quality of service may be high under
   higher advertising prices to merchants.          concentration · large platforms can fund
 • Passed through to consumers as higher            innovation.
   prices for goods advertised on platforms.      • Advertisers, not end consumers, pay
 • Reduced allocative efficiency: P > MC on         directly · incidence question.
   advertising services.                          • Emergence of TikTok (6.7%) shows market
 • Consumers pay a zero monetary price for          can be partly contestable even when
   headline services (Extract A) · welfare          concentrated.
   harm hidden in data extraction, attention      • Judgement: net consumer-welfare effect is
   costs.                                           ambiguous · quality/innovation gains
 • Dynamic-efficiency benefits (large R&D           mean concentration ≠ automatic detriment.
   budgets funded by ad revenue) may                Regulation should focus on specific
   partially offset static losses.                  conduct (self-preferencing, exclusionary
                                                    practices) rather than structure alone.


6(c) · Do data-portability obligations reduce barriers to entry? (8 marks)
Indicative content (KAA 5 / Eval 3):
Accept two developed factors, e.g.:
1. Portability reduces switching costs · users can move transaction history, preferences, content
to a rival (Extract B). Chain: lowers lock-in → incentivises entry → more contestable market (2–3).
2. Interoperability enables challenger platforms to access the incumbent's user base without
reproducing full scale (2).
3. Possible effect on minimum efficient scale · rival can reach MES sooner with portable data (1–
2).
Evaluation (3): Critics note network effects and proprietary algorithms (Extract B) remain the
dominant source of lock-in, not data; mandated interoperability may reduce incumbents' incentive
to invest in security and innovation; empirical evidence mixed (PSD2 Open Banking in UK took 5+

9EC0/01 · Advanced · Mark Scheme                                                      Page 6 of 10
years to reach meaningful adoption). Judgement: portability is a necessary but not sufficient
condition for contestability · must be combined with conduct rules and structural remedies.

6(d) · Case for SMS designation under 2024 Act (10 marks)
            KAA (AO1 + AO2 + AO3)                               Evaluation (AO4)

 • Case FOR: dominant platforms have              • Case AGAINST: risk of regulatory capture;
   durable market power (e.g. Google 91.2%          UK regime uniquely prescriptive may
   of search · Fig 1) that persistent market        discourage investment in UK-facing
   forces do not erode.                             services (Extract C).
 • Tailored conduct requirements target           • Definition and scope of 'Strategic Market
   specific abuses: self-preferencing, tying,       Status' is subjective · legal challenges
   access denial.                                   likely.
 • Fines up to 10% of global turnover provide     • Dynamic innovation may be chilled if
   credible enforcement.                            platforms cannot leverage data or scale.
 • SMS regime is ex-ante (prevention) rather      • Competition from new entrants (TikTok
   than ex-post (remedial) · avoids lengthy         6.7% · Fig 2) may already be disciplining
   competition cases.                               incumbents.
 • Consumer groups support · promise of           • Judgement: designation is justified for
   lower commission rates, easier switching         entrenched positions with weak
   (Extract C).                                     competitive pressure (general search); less
                                                    clear for markets already facing rivalry (e-
                                                    commerce). Approach should be evidence-
                                                    based, market-by-market, with
                                                    proportionate remedies.


6(e) · Adequacy of traditional market-power measures (15 marks)
            KAA (AO1 + AO2 + AO3)                               Evaluation (AO4)

 • Define CRn, HHI: static concentration          • Traditional measures miss the 'competition
   measures based on market share.                  for the market' nature of tipping markets
 • HHI = sum of squared market shares;              (Bertrand competition in winner-takes-all
   thresholds: <1000 unconcentrated, 1000–          episodes).
   1800 moderately, >1800 highly                  • HHI doesn't capture data moats or
   concentrated.                                    switching costs.
 • Applying to Fig 2 digital advertising: HHI ≈   • Market share of a free service is not the
   39.7² + 24.2² + 11.4² + 6.7² + 3.7² +            same as pricing power.
   (distributed) ≈ 2,400 · highly                 • Newer approaches: SSNDQ (small
   concentrated.                                    significant non-transitory decrease in
 • Strengths: simple, transparent, comparable       quality), access to data, attention share.
   across markets; trigger for further            • Complementary qualitative assessments:
   investigation.                                   entry analysis, R&D intensity, complaint
 • Limitations in digital: static measures miss     volumes.
   multi-sidedness, network effects, dynamic      • Judgement: traditional measures remain a
   entry, zero-price services.                      useful first screen but are insufficient on
 • Market definition harder: ad spending vs.        their own in digital markets. They need to
   search vs. mobile OS · are these                 be supplemented with multi-dimensional
   separate markets?                                assessments capturing dynamic effects,
                                                    non-price competition, data access and
                                                    network effects. The 2024 Act's SMS
                                                    regime reflects this recognition.

                Section C · Question 7 OR Question 8 (25 marks)
Question 7 · Does microeconomic theory explain digital markets? (25
marks)
            KAA (AO1 + AO2 + AO3)                                  Evaluation (AO4)

 • Traditional theories deployed: perfect            • Arguments for inadequacy: zero-price
   competition, monopoly, oligopoly (Cournot,          models break standard welfare analysis;
   Bertrand, kinked demand), monopolistic              consumer pays in attention and data, not
   competition.                                        money.
 • Relevant concepts: scale economies, entry         • Multi-sidedness: conventional MR = MC
   barriers, contestability, price discrimination.     rule must account for externalities across
 • Diagrams: monopoly vs. perfect                      sides of the platform.
   competition welfare comparison; kinked            • Dynamic contestability: 'competition for the
   demand for oligopolistic tacit collusion.           market' rather than 'in the market' ·
 • Digital markets exhibit: near-zero MC,              Schumpeterian creative destruction.
   strong network effects, two/multi-sided           • Network effects and data feedback loops
   platforms, data as input, algorithmic               not well captured by static models.
   pricing.                                          • Behavioural economics: consumers
 • Examples: Google in search, Amazon in e-            systematically irrational · affects demand-
   commerce, Apple App Store, Uber/food-               curve stability.
   delivery platforms, Netflix/SVOD.                 • Judgement: existing theory explains a
 • Arguments for adequacy: core concepts               significant portion of digital market
   still apply (entry barriers, MC = MR), rent         behaviour but is incomplete. Augmentation
   extraction visible in profit margins.               with two-sided market theory (Rochet-
                                                       Tirole), behavioural insights, and
                                                       attention/data-based welfare frameworks is
                                                       required. Not a rejection of theory · an
                                                       extension.


Question 8 · Behavioural exploitation and government intervention (25
marks)
            KAA (AO1 + AO2 + AO3)                                  Evaluation (AO4)

 • Concepts: bounded rationality, anchoring,         • Counter: not all consumers are irrational;
   default bias, availability heuristic, loss          firms respond to revealed demand and
   aversion, present bias.                             competition; diversity of consumer types.
 • Examples of firm exploitation: drip pricing       • Behavioural insights still debated ·
   (Ryanair, Booking.com), dark patterns               replication crisis in some studies; effect
   (subscription traps), choice overload at            sizes often modest.
   checkout, reference-price framing ('was           • Over-intervention risks paternalism, limits
   £50, now £29').                                     choice, raises compliance costs.
 • Market-failure framing: asymmetric                • Markets already provide some self-
   information + cognitive bias → allocative           correction: review platforms, price-
   inefficiency, reduced consumer surplus.             comparison sites, consumer advocacy.
 • Government intervention options:                  • Alternative: lighter-touch nudges (auto-
   disclosure rules, default settings (nudges),        enrol out of dark patterns, default privacy
   direct prohibition of dark patterns,                settings) rather than prohibition.
   enforcement of price transparency.
                                                     • Judgement: evidence strongly supports
                                                       SOME intervention in markets where dark-
                                                       pattern exploitation is systematic

9EC0/01 · Advanced · Mark Scheme                                                          Page 9 of 10
            KAA (AO1 + AO2 + AO3)                           Evaluation (AO4)

                                                 (gambling, subscription services).
                                                 'Extensive' intervention goes too far ·
                                                 intervention should be proportionate,
                                                 targeted, and evidence-based. Rejection of
                                                 full paternalistic framing; acceptance of
                                                 selective regulation.
